sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: RIGHT and UP both had no effect. Squares at (41, 21) and (46, 36). Movement seems position-dependent - each square only responds to certain directions at certain locations.
- Recent findings: Blue squares moved from row 51 to row 46 (up 5 rows again). They're moving up consistently through the black shape's "legs".
- Plan: Try DOWN to bring left square back to row 46 so both squares align, then UP might move both.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 49 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN moved the left square from (41, 21) to (46, 21), but the right square moved from (46, 36) to (51, 36). So DOWN moves both squares down! The right square was at row 46 and moved to row 51.
So the pattern is:
- UP moves squares up (by 5 rows)
- DOWN moves squares down (by 5 rows)
- RIGHT moves squares right (by 5 cols)
- LEFT presumably moves squares left (by 5 cols)
But movement is constrained by the black shape. Squares can only move through the "openings" in the legs.
Current state: left square at (46, 21), right square at (51, 36).
The left square is at the opening of the left leg (row 46). The right square is at the bottom of the right leg (row 51).
To move both up together, they need to be at the same row. Let me bring the right square up to row 46 first by using UP. But wait, will UP move both or just one?
Let me think about the mechanics more carefully:
- When both were at row 46, UP moved both to row 41
- When left was at 41 and right at 36, UP only moved left (from 41 to... wait, no, UP didn't move anything)
- When left was at 41 and right at 36, RIGHT didn't move anything
- When left was at 41 and right at 36, DOWN moved left to 46 and right to 51
So DOWN moved both squares down. UP didn't work when they were at different rows. RIGHT didn't work either.
Hmm, so maybe directional moves only work when both squares are at the same row/column? Let me check:
- Initially both at row 51: UP moved both to row 46 ✓
- Both at row 46: RIGHT moved right square from col 41 to 36... wait, did it move both? Let me check history.
Actually I need to look at the history more carefully. Let me check what happened when RIGHT was executed.
Looking at the images and the history:
- Step 44: both blues at bottom (row 51)
- Step 45: both moved up (row 46) - after UP
- Step 46: right square moved right... no wait, the right square was at col 41 and moved to col 36 after RIGHT. But did the left square move too?
Looking at step 45 image: left blue at left leg, right blue at right leg
Looking at step 46 image: same positions... hmm
Actually I think I need to re-examine. Let me check the history frames.
The key finding is: DOWN moved both squares. So maybe UP will also move both squares when they're at different rows? Let me try UP now.
Current: left at (46, 21), right at (51, 36)
If I do UP, maybe both move up 5 rows: left to (41, 21), right to (46, 36)
That would put them back to where they were before DOWN. That's not progress.
Maybe I need to think about this differently. The goal might be to get both squares to specific positions. Let me look at the colored regions:
- Yellow region is on the left side of the black shape
- Orange region is on the right side
Maybe the goal is to move both squares into the colored regions (yellow and orange). The left square at (41, 21) is in the yellow region. The right square needs to get to the orange region.
The orange region seems to be above row 46 in the right leg. So the right square needs to go UP from row 46 to row 41.
But the problem is that UP only works when both squares are at the same row. Currently they're at different rows.
Let me try: bring both to row 46 first, then UP.
- Left is at (46, 21) - already at row 46
- Right is at (51, 36) - need to move it up to row 46
But how to move just the right square? Maybe I need to move the left square somewhere else first, then move the right square, then move the left square back?
Or maybe I should try UP now and see what happens. If UP moves both, then left goes to 41 and right goes to 46. Then I can try RIGHT to move the right square... but RIGHT didn't work before at (46, 36).
Wait, let me re-examine. When I did RIGHT earlier, the squares were at (41, 21) and (46, 36). RIGHT didn't work. But when both were at row 46, RIGHT moved the right square from col 41 to 36.
So maybe RIGHT only works when both squares are at the same row AND at the right columns?
Let me try a different approach. Let me get both squares to row 46, then try RIGHT.
Current: left at (46, 21), right at (51, 36)
I need to move right square up. Let me try UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: DOWN moved both squares - left from (41,21) to (46,21), right from (46,36) to (51,36). Directional moves affect both squares but are constrained by the black shape openings. Movement is 5 cells per step.
Plan: Try UP to move both squares up - left to (41,21), right to (46,36). Then try RIGHT when both are at different positions to see if it works.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try UP to move both squares up
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blues = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary'])
        blues.append((center_r, center_c))
blues.sort()
print(f"After UP: blues at {blues}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After UP: blues at [(41.0, 21.0), (46.0, 36.0)]
Done: False
